_comment: Backup of the claude.ai cloud routine that produces waits.csv. This is the exact body accepted by the RemoteTrigger API (POST /v1/code/triggers). See ../README.md for how to recreate the routine from this file, and for the three environment/permission settings that are NOT captured here and must be configured by hand.
name: USS Battlestar Galactica Wait Times

message.content:
Record the current wait times for the two Battlestar Galactica roller coasters at Universal Studios Singapore, and append them to `waits.csv` in the checked-out repo (https://github.com/huiminli1998/uss-wait-times).

## Step 1 — Fetch the data

    curl -s --max-time 30 'https://api.themeparks.wiki/v1/entity/f95d7f76-2024-4510-b799-26e122d0e448/live'

This domain is on the environment's Custom network allowlist, so it should succeed. The response has a top-level `liveData` array.

## Step 2 — Extract the two rides

Find these two entries in `liveData` by their exact `id`:

- `7b8c7a5b-a4fa-4c74-ac85-6ba72fd653bb` — record as ride `HUMAN`
- `ccab7e24-6848-4f71-9b2e-459bba5831a0` — record as ride `CYLON`

For each, read `status`, `queue.STANDBY.waitTime`, and `lastUpdated`.

Parse the JSON programmatically (`jq` is installed). Never invent, estimate, or eyeball a wait time — only record a number you actually parsed out of the response.

IMPORTANT: when a ride is closed or under refurbishment, `queue` or `queue.STANDBY.waitTime` may be missing or null. That is normal — leave `wait_time` EMPTY in the CSV and keep the real `status` value. Do not treat it as an error and do not skip the run.

## Step 3 — Append to waits.csv

`waits.csv` already exists in the repo with this header:

    fetched_at_sgt,ride,status,wait_time,last_updated

Append exactly two rows — HUMAN first, then CYLON. Compute `fetched_at_sgt` yourself with `date` (Singapore is UTC+8) and use the SAME timestamp for both rows of this run, so the pair can be grouped later:

    2026-07-16T14:23:00+08:00,HUMAN,OPERATING,100,2026-07-16T06:19:16.647Z
    2026-07-16T14:23:00+08:00,CYLON,OPERATING,80,2026-07-16T06:16:46.682Z

Those values are an EXAMPLE of the shape only — use the real values you fetched. A closed ride looks like:

    2026-07-16T14:23:00+08:00,CYLON,CLOSED,,2026-07-16T06:16:46.682Z

Ensure the file ends with a trailing newline, and never rewrite or reorder existing rows — append only.

## Step 4 — Commit and push

    git add waits.csv
    git -c user.name='USS Wait Times Routine' -c user.email='noreply@anthropic.com' commit -m 'waits: <SGT timestamp> — HUMAN <n>, CYLON <n>'
    git push origin HEAD:main

If the push is rejected as non-fast-forward, run `git pull --rebase origin main` and push again.

## Step 5 — Report

Output one line: the SGT timestamp, HUMAN wait + status, CYLON wait + status, and the pushed commit SHA.

## On failure

If the curl fails, returns non-JSON, or neither ride id is present, STILL append two rows for this run with status `FETCH_ERROR`, empty `wait_time`, empty `last_updated`:

    2026-07-16T14:23:00+08:00,HUMAN,FETCH_ERROR,,
    2026-07-16T14:23:00+08:00,CYLON,FETCH_ERROR,,

Commit with message `waits: <SGT timestamp> — FETCH_ERROR (<exact reason, e.g. HTTP status or proxy error text>)` and push.

Do NOT fall back to WebFetch or any other tool to work around a fetch failure. A loud, visible FETCH_ERROR row is the correct outcome — a wrong number recorded as if it were real is far worse than a recorded gap. Never exit silently without recording the run.
